results.possibleIdentity: Stanley Froud was an electronics engineer, consultant, and editor based in New York City, active from the early 1960s through at least the late 1980s. He served as an assistant editor and regional editor for 'Electronics' magazine (a McGraw-Hill publication) in the early 1960s. By 1971, he was working as an electronics consultant and resided at 817 West End Avenue in New York. During this period, he contributed technical expertise to the 'Amateur Scientist' column in Scientific American, where he detailed the use of differential operational amplifiers to simulate physical systems such as levers, cams, and clocks. His work involved the design and application of discrete and integrated circuit components, including transistors and silicon wafers. Froud's technical interests also extended to computer ergonomics; in 1989, he communicated with Macworld magazine regarding furniture and hardware setups to minimize health issues related to long-term computer use. His profile as an electronics expert in New York during the late 1960s and early 1970s is consistent with the technical members who collaborated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.
